topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure, mocking me from the mail slot, felt like a personal insult. I slammed the heavy envelope onto the worn kitchen counter, the sound echoing sharply in the small space. "Condo?" I spat the word out, tasting the bitterness of it. I paced the cramped living room, my jaw tight, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. Every picture of sleek, minimalist interiors felt like a deliberate erasure of everything familiar—the chipped paint on the hallway trim, the smell of old coffee clinging to the carpet. I stopped by the window, glaring down at the street, feeling a hot flush creep up my chest. This wasn't an upgrade; it was an eviction disguised as an opportunity. I grabbed my phone, my thumb hovering over the number for the building manager, ready to unleash a torrent of pointed questions.

[story 2]

I stared at the lease renewal documents spread across the dining table, the fine print swimming before my eyes. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles whitening as I tried to keep the tremor out of my voice. "They can't just do this," I muttered, the words coming out low and strained. The realization that this familiar, slightly shabby apartment was slated for a radical overhaul made my stomach churn. I walked over to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, breathing in deeply, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my heart. It felt like a violation, a stripping away of my established space. I paced back and forth, a tight, restless energy vibrating beneath my skin, every movement sharp and jerky.

[story 3]

When the developer’s notice finally landed on my door, I didn't bother opening it immediately. I stood by the entryway, my breath coming in short, shallow gasps. The sheer audacity of the plan made my vision narrow. I kicked lightly at the base of the doorframe, a frustrated, sharp sound escaping my throat. I turned away from the door, walking straight to the small kitchenette and gripping the edge of the counter until my fingers ached. I looked around the room—at the peeling wallpaper, the mismatched furniture—and felt a profound sense of injustice wash over me. This wasn't a fresh start; it felt like being shoved out of my own life. I ran a hand roughly through my hair, the motion jerky and uncontrolled.
